museum.name: Gaming Time Machine
museum.curatorNote: Initial researched draft for May 1996. Dates are deliberately conservative where regional timing is uncertain.

sections.title: News
sections.subtitle: Five researched month markers or context notes.

items.title: Mario 64 and N64 coverage builds before Japan's launch.
items.body: A month marker with regional timing kept visible rather than smoothed into a single global date.
items.sourceNote: See source notes for this exhibit; exact dates are used only when the reviewed sources support them.

items.title: Quake is nearly ready to redraw PC expectations.
items.body: The network is becoming part of the exhibit, not just a footnote to boxed software.
items.sourceNote: See source notes for this exhibit; exact dates are used only when the reviewed sources support them.

items.title: PlayStation owners look toward Tomb Raider, Crash and Resident Evil.
items.body: A researched period marker, written cautiously where sources disagree or the month is more atmosphere than hard date.
items.sourceNote: See source notes for this exhibit; exact dates are used only when the reviewed sources support them.

items.title: Saturn owners still follow arcade conversions and imports.
items.body: A researched period marker, written cautiously where sources disagree or the month is more atmosphere than hard date.
items.sourceNote: See source notes for this exhibit; exact dates are used only when the reviewed sources support them.

items.title: The month is mainly a preview-room exhibit.
items.body: A researched period marker, written cautiously where sources disagree or the month is more atmosphere than hard date.

sections.title: Releases
sections.subtitle: Eight notable releases, led by month-specific anchors where evidence supports them.

items.title: Duke Nukem 3D
items.body: 3D Realms gives the post-Doom FPS a louder, cruder, more recognisable cityscape.
items.sourceNote: Late January/shareware and February references vary; month is treated cautiously.

items.title: Pokemon Red and Green
items.body: Game Freak begins a Japanese handheld phenomenon that will not reach Europe until 1999.
items.sourceNote: Japanese Red/Green date is widely cited.

items.title: Civilization II
items.body: MicroProse refines the empire spreadsheet into one-more-turn ritual.
items.sourceNote: February 1996 is commonly cited.

items.title: Resident Evil
items.body: Capcom gives PlayStation a mansion full of fixed cameras, awkward doors and survival dread.
items.sourceNote: Japanese date is source-cited; Europe follows in August.

items.title: Quake
items.body: id Software makes true 3D, mouselook and network play feel like the new PC frontier.
items.sourceNote: Steam lists June 22, 1996.

items.title: Super Mario 64
items.body: Nintendo teaches a generation how to move in 3D space.
items.sourceNote: MobyGames lists June 23, 1996.

items.title: Crash Bandicoot
items.body: Naughty Dog gives Sony a corridor-platform mascot with UK retail reach in November.
items.sourceNote: North America September 9; Europe November 8.

items.title: Tomb Raider
items.body: Core Design turns exploration, acrobatics and Lara Croft into a British export story.
items.sourceNote: UK/Europe date is source-noted; some sources conflict for earlier European timing.

sections.title: Hardware
sections.subtitle: Machines, media and platform context around the month.

items.title: Nintendo 64 launches in Japan and North America
items.body: The N64 makes 3D movement feel tactile, though UK players wait until 1997.
items.sourceNote: Japan June 23 and North America September 29 are widely cited; PAL follows in 1997.

items.title: PlayStation becomes the UK default future
items.body: Sony's machine benefits from CD pricing, demo discs and a growing identity beyond children's toys.
items.sourceNote: UK retail and magazine context.

items.title: Saturn fights with arcade credibility
items.body: Sega still has Virtua Fighter, Sega Rally and import mystique, but the platform story is harder to explain.
items.sourceNote: Saturn/UK magazine context.

items.title: PC 3D acceleration starts to matter
items.body: Quake, 3D cards and network play make hardware knowledge part of PC gaming status.
items.sourceNote: 1996 PC hardware context.

sections.title: Online Life
sections.subtitle: How network play, downloads, fan pages and demo culture felt at the time.

items.title: Quake makes servers feel like places
items.body: For PC players, multiplayer begins to mean IP addresses, server lists and low-ping envy.

items.title: Shareware memory still matters
items.body: Duke Nukem 3D and Quake live partly through episodes, patches and files passed around outside shops.

items.title: Magazine discs remain the everyday internet
items.body: For many UK players, a cover disc is still the most reliable download.

items.title: Early web pages feel handmade
items.body: Game information online exists, but it sits beside BBS habits, FTP folders and print.

sections.title: What It Felt Like
sections.subtitle: A short atmospheric reading from the player side of history.

items.title: 3D stopped being a promise
items.body: Mario, Lara and Quake made space feel like the new medium.

items.title: The PlayStation looked adult
items.body: CD cases, demo discs and black plastic made games feel closer to music retail.

items.title: Britain watched the N64 through glass
items.body: The N64 existed elsewhere, but UK players mostly met it as screenshots and import prices.

items.title: The PC became a machine you tuned
items.body: Getting the future meant drivers, memory, sound cards and eventually 3D hardware.

editorNotes: Initial AI-generated research draft for May 1996.
Month-specific dates are used only where reviewed sources support them; broad market context is marked in certainty/sourceNote fields.
Magazine cover images are reconstructed placeholders and should be replaced with verified/licensed scans if used editorially.
